Hey guys, welcome back. I just want to give you a little bit of an example breakdown for the Sarcastic Sally. So we had two theme plates. The first one, theme plate A over here. Our example is from Tenix Emails Mastery. So this was the email that really inspired the template it got sent out in back-to-back launches performing really well each time. So here we go. So Battlefield we open up with the biggest objection against you, your industry, or your product. And we start off with, yeah, a little self deprecating. Bet you heard email is dead. Murdered by messenger bots. Killed by AI. Replaced by telepathy. So here we're listing the so-called alternatives that aren't actually alternatives. But we're being sarcastic about it. The last one is on the side of being a little more absurd or exaggerated. So even like ten words in people know we're absolutely being sarcastic. And it's like, good rins right? Email is so bad. The worst. I mean with email you have to get this. You have to sit down, open convert kit or intercom or whatever, and type out messages to groups of people who signed up to hear from you. And then you have to wait like sometimes whole minutes for those first notifications from PayPal and Stripe. Payment received. Ugh gross. So here we're taking that moment of highest pleasure, which is sending an email, you know, very specific through convert kit and getting this payment notification. So that's the moment of highest pleasure, but we're framing it as a negative sarcastically. Gross messenger bots. Now there's tech I can get behind. So now we're contrasting it with the impracticalities of those so-called alternatives, but framing those as positives. And AI, I mean, that's the future. Sure, it's still far in the future, but you know, it'll get there. And when that happens, you'll just create a card and Trello called activate AI to do campaign and voila, nothing artificial, but those results. I mean, unless you count that they don't actually exist yet. But back on topic, email is the worst. And now we're framing our testimonials as negative while being sarcastic. It took this one copywriter named Paranormalic two emails to sell at almost $20,000 worth of product-ized services. Yes, two, like, like why not one, right? Email. And Marian Chimbarri only made half a million dollars for teachable with the emails she wrote for them. I mean, how are you supposed to be valued at a billion dollars by this time next year if you keep chipping away at things half millions at a time? But I guess if that's your thing, so now we're breaking frame. If you like the value proposition of directly accessing a list of prospects anytime of day with any offer you're promoting using tech you've already got access to and don't need to hire 19 year old to explain to you than K Cool. Sure, maybe emails not dead for you. In which case you should join before 4pm today. PS and this is our play it safe PS, which is really a sincere, sincere argument, arguing against that opening objective that email is dead. Oh, email is not dead for you. Cool. Yeah, it seems to me my clients and the people asking me for referrals to great copyrights that emails in fact anything but a member of the White Walker army, it's live, it's kicking and strapping extra zeros into the stripe accounts of the startup matters, growth marketers, online entrepreneurs and talented copywriters who know how to tap to its conversion. Power email has survived a thousand next big things coming and going. You're reading this email right now and whether you hit an email or loved it, you're reading it. So you just go on arguing for the fact that email is actually still alive. So as you'll notice sarcastic tone all the way throughout, it starts to soften in that final call to action. We're still a little bit sarcastic saying, I guess if that's your thing, but we are starting to flip it into a little bit more of a sincere tone and then the play it safe PS. So that is version one, version two is really about framing the moment of highest tension as something desirable or saying, do you want the moment of highest tension? This is how to do it. So come out right out of the gates, want to have the world's most mediocre freelance copyrighting career. It's easy. Hang up a shingle, roll out a bed at 11 a.m. work in your SpongeBob slippers and maintain an inner sense of satisfaction that you've transcended the matrix and live a better life than your troglodyte friends who are still slogging it out for the man. So we're just essentially giving that whole moment of highest tension sarcastically and this is the path towards achieving it. And then we go here, see mediocrity is easy and the rewards are awesome. So now we're continuing to frame. So this is the path to achieving the mediocre career. This is what the so-called rewards are. So we're still being sarcastic pretending those are desirable. You get to complain sometimes in gift form. You get to secretly hit the copywriters at the top. Damn, Illuminati, you get to do easy and safe things and never trigger a single droplet of cortisol until your breaks need fixing and you realize you've only built enough to cover your rent and groceries and Luke isn't willing to do you solid. If that's all you want out of the freelance life, read no further, pull up a snuggy and continue Netflixing your way into good enough. But if you've earned, if you've armed yourself with some deft copyrighting skills, have some real wins under your belt and want to see just how far you can take your freelance career in 2019, then I border on line insist that you save your seat here. So this one sentence here is exclusively empowering them to take that action. You know, they have skills, they have some real wins and they're ambitious enough. They really want to see how far they could take this. We're breaking the frame here. We're saying if that's what you want, cool. You know, you're disqualified from this product. Just keep doing those things. This is the path to the mediocre life and that works for you. If you have that higher standard, this is the path to take and that final close and the things we're mentioning here are direct opposites to the things here. So that is it. Those are two ways to go about the sarcastic Sally. The important thing is that once you're really committing to the tone, committing to the frame, you really hold that frame for the duration of the email until it is time to be sincere in your final call to actions. So that's it. Give this one a go. It's a great way to really get attention and focus on that moment of highest pleasure and of how you stack up against alternatives or to overcome objections that people might have about your product, your industry or your product category.
